SQL & Aggregate Functions

It has Movies, Actors, Casting (plus other tables)

MovieActor Entity
If we 'reverse engineer' the move database, we can create an ERD.
It has many-to-one relationships.
The Identifier of MOVIEACTOR is MovieNo + ActorNo.
CASTING is a WEAK entity.
The two relationship names are underlined
The casting entity has a double outline
The identifier of casting is movieNo + ActorNo
How do you know this?
The ERD fully explains this information

MovieActor / Casting Entity
The naming of weak entities can sometimes be difficult.
The name 'MovieActor' is not a natural or 'real world' name.
A more appropriate name might be CASTING
Wherever possible, try to use the word or term that would be used within the industry/business that you are modeling

The completed ERD
Answer these questions:
Once the ERD has been converted
to a database schema
Which relations will have FKs?
How many FKs in each relation?

SQL Aggregate Functions
Previously, you have used Aggregate functions
Called "Totals" in MS Access.
These used functions such as Count, Sum, Min, Max …
Each function is applied over a number of rows at one time
The usual SELECT statement clauses such as WHERE and ORDER BY can be used in the same way

SQL COUNT() Function
COUNT(*) is an example of an aggregate function
It counts the number of rows
Use a * with this statement to count ALL rows
Use a <column-name> with this statement to count ALL rows that do not contain a NULL value in that column
SELECT	COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
SELECT	COUNT(ratingcode)
FROM 	movie
Three movies have a NULL ratingcode
COUNT(*)
384
COUNT(ratingcode)
381
If ratingcode does not contain Nulls, both statements produce identical results

SQL Aggregate Functions
The Select clause still has all the usual clauses available.
These examples use a WHERE clause
Only rows that meet the condition are Counted
SELECT	COUNT(*) FROM movie
WHERE  relyear = 2004;
SELECT	COUNT(*) FROM movie
WHERE  relyear >= 2000
AND       relyear <= 2009;
COUNT(*)
19
COUNT(*)
117

SQL Aggregate Functions
This is a list of the most widely used aggregate functions
In each case the function is applied over a number of rows
• Sum	• Count	• Avg	• Min	• Max
When using Sum, Avg, Min & Max you must specify a column name
SELECT	SUM( run_time ) FROM movie ;
SELECT	AVG( run_time ) FROM movie;
SELECT	MIN( run_time ) FROM movie ;
SELECT	MAX( run_time ) FROM movie ;
SELECT	SUM( runtime ) FROM movie WHERE UPPER(title) = 'HAMLET';
SELECT	MIN( run_time ) FROM movie WHERE year = 2005;

SQL Round Function
When dealing with averages or when dividing numbers, results often contain a large number of digits after the decimal place
The Round() function is useful in this situation.
Syntax:
ROUND ( number, [ decimal_places ] )
If the number of decimal places is omitted, then the round function will round the number to 0 decimal places.
SELECT   AVG( runtime ) FROM movie
SELECT   ROUND( AVG (runtime ), 2) FROM movie
SELECT   ROUND( AVG (runtime ) ) AS "AVG_RUNTIME"
FROM     movie
AVG(RUNTIME)
121.5094339622641509433
ROUND(AVG(RUNTIME),2)
121.51
AVG_RUNTIME
122

SQL Aggregate vs Non-aggregate
Items listed in the Select Clause of a SELECT statement
are generally either
Non-aggregate expressions (Typically columns names)
TITLE
RUNTIME
RELYEAR
MOVIENO…
SQL Aggregate expressions:
AVG(RUNTIME)
MIN(RELYEAR)
SUM(RUNTIME)
COUNT(*)…
